abstract: This article provides a comprehensive review of the current state-of-the-art in overlapping community detection, focusing on algorithms, quality metrics, and benchmarking standards. We conduct a rigorous comparative analysis of fourteen prominent algorithms. Beyond traditional community-level evaluations, we introduce a novel framework designed to assess an algorithm's precision in identifying overlapping nodes, specifically accounting for issues of overdetection and underdetection. By integrating community-level performance—measured via normalized mutual information and the Omega index—with node-level accuracy measured by the F-score, several key findings emerge. In networks with low overlapping density, SLPA, OSLOM, Game, and COPRA consistently outperform their competitors. In contrast, for networks characterized by high overlapping density and diversity, SLPA and Game demonstrate the most stable performance; however, our results indicate that accurately resolving structures in such complex networks remains an open challenge. Finally, our analysis of real-world networks reveals a consistent structural pattern: overlapping nodes typically constitute a small fraction of the network (generally under 30%) and rarely membership in more than two or three communities.
